title: Memory Addresses
description: What an address actually is, why it's shown in hex, sizeof, and how array elements sit at addresses spaced exactly by their type's size.
objectives: Understand a memory address as a numeric location in memory
Know addresses are conventionally displayed in hexadecimal
Use sizeof to see how many bytes a type occupies
Understand array elements are stored contiguously, addresses spaced by sizeof(type)

sections.text: We've used & to get an address since Pointers, without examining what an address really is. Let's look closer.
A memory address is a number identifying a specific location in a computer's memory — every byte of memory has its own unique address.

Recall What Happens When You Run an Executable from Source Code and Executable Files: the operating system loads a running program into memory. Every variable in that program occupies some number of bytes at some specific address within that memory.

That 0x... value is a hexadecimal (base-16) number — the conventional way addresses are displayed, since it's a more compact way to write large numbers than decimal. You don't need to understand hexadecimal deeply here — just recognize that 0x... means "this is an address."

Unlike a value like 25, an address printed by your program will typically be different each time you run it — modern operating systems deliberately place programs at different memory locations for security reasons. Never write code (or examples) that depend on a specific address value staying the same between runs.
5. sizeof — How Many Bytes a Type Occupies

sizeof(Type) returns the number of bytes that type occupies — matching the typical sizes previewed back in Data Types. These exact numbers can technically vary by platform, but 4, 1, and 8 are the common values on most modern systems.
6. Array Elements Sit Right Next to Each Other

Recall Arrays: elements are stored together as one contiguous block. That's not just a mental model — arr[1]'s address is genuinely arr[0]'s address plus sizeof(int) bytes, and arr[2]'s address is another sizeof(int) further along.
7. Proving the Spacing (Without Depending on Exact Addresses)

Subtracting two pointers of the same type gives the number of elements between them, not raw bytes — this works out to 1 and 2 no matter what the actual addresses happen to be on a given run, because C++ automatically accounts for sizeof(int) when doing this kind of pointer arithmetic.

sections.title: House numbers on a street
sections.text: Memory addresses are like house numbers on a long street — every house (byte) has a unique number, and houses belonging to the same family (an array's elements) sit at consecutive numbers, spaced by exactly how wide each house is (sizeof the type). A family occupying 4-unit-wide houses starting at number 100 would have their next house at 104, then 108 — exactly what sizeof(int) spacing looks like for consecutive array elements.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming an address stays the same across different runs of a program — it usually doesn't, and code should never depend on a specific address value.
Treating the 0x... printed address as if it were meaningful decimal data — it's hexadecimal, a different number base, purely a location identifier.
Forgetting sizeof depends on the type — sizeof(int) and sizeof(double) are typically different.
Assuming pointer subtraction gives a byte count — subtracting two same-type pointers gives an element count, since C++ divides by sizeof(type) automatically.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a memory address?
questions.answer: A number that uniquely identifies a specific location in a computer's memory.

questions.question: In what number base are memory addresses conventionally displayed?
questions.answer: Hexadecimal (base-16), typically shown with a 0x prefix.

questions.question: What does sizeof(Type) return?
questions.answer: The number of bytes that type occupies in memory.

questions.question: How are the elements of an array arranged in memory?
questions.answer: Contiguously — stored back to back, with each element's address exactly sizeof(elementType) bytes after the previous one.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A memory address is a number identifying a specific location in memory, conventionally displayed in hexadecimal and typically different on every run of a program, since operating systems place programs at varying memory locations. sizeof(Type) reveals how many bytes a given type occupies — commonly 4 for int, 1 for char, 8 for double. Array elements are stored contiguously, meaning each element's address is exactly sizeof(elementType) bytes after the previous one, which is why subtracting two same-type pointers gives a clean element count rather than a raw byte difference.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Memory Addresses
sections.text: Address = house number on a street. sizeof = how wide each house is. Array elements = houses of the same family, lined up back to back.
